data.area: Analytical/quantitative core: FAIR-lite scales, 3-lens scoring + deterministic aggregation, quant model (baseline/effectiveness/residual/aggregate/marginal), and advanced analytics (Monte Carlo, tornado, EAL/ROSI, framework sensitivity).

findings.feature: EAL / ROSI (advanced_analytics.py eal_rosi + outputs/08 + dashboard headline)
findings.problem: 'Expected Annual Loss' is computed on the baseline MODE, not the mean of the distribution the same script commits to. Every baseline triangular is right-skewed (mode sits nearer the low bound), so the mode systematically understates the true expected value. eal_base = p['b'][1]/100*loss uses index [1] = mode.
findings.evidence: Line 190-191: `eal_base = p['b'][1] / 100 * loss` and `eal_resid = p['b'][1] * (1 - p['e'][1] / 100) / 100 * loss`. Docstring line 294 admits 'point estimates on mode values' but never flags the resulting downward bias. Measured bias: triangular means vs modes give R3 20->22.7%, R4 15->17.7%, R5 10->11.7%; portfolio baseline EAL rises from $2.36M (mode) to $2.68M (mean), a 13.6% understatement (up to 17.8% for R4).
findings.fix: Compute EAL on the triangular mean (low+mode+high)/3 (or reuse the MC mean the script already produces) instead of the mode; or rename to avoid the word 'Expected' and state explicitly that EAL is a mode-point estimate biased low by ~10-18%. The bias is conservative (understates avoided loss, so ROSI is understated too), so this is a credibility/labeling fix, not a direction-of-decision fix.
findings.demo_risk: A sharp quant evaluator will immediately notice that a file running a 100k-iteration Monte Carlo (which yields the mean for free) then computes 'Expected' Annual Loss on the mode of an admittedly skewed distribution. It reads as not understanding that E[loss] uses the mean, undercutting the most numerate part of the submission.

findings.feature: Prioritization-framework sensitivity: impact-weight sweep
findings.problem: The impact-weight sweep only moves the primary weight UP (0.50 -> 0.70), never below 0.50. It therefore never stress-tests the arguably more decision-relevant hypothesis that SECONDARY impact (notification, fines, churn, SOC 2 setback on 500k-PII data) dominates. R2 and R3 both have secondary median (5) > primary (3), so a leader who weights secondary at 0.6-0.7 is not covered, yet the output presents 'top_risk_stable_across_sweep: true' as a robustness result.
findings.evidence: Line 220: `for w in (0.50, 0.55, 0.60, 0.65, 0.70)`. ASSUMPTIONS.md justifies 60/40 as 'direct loss is more certain than secondary loss' — a defensible prior, but the sensitivity only probes the direction that reinforces it. R2 medians primary_impact=3, secondary_impact=5; R3 primary=4, secondary=5 (outputs/02).
findings.fix: Extend the sweep to at least 0.30-0.40 primary weight (secondary-dominant) and report whether R3 still leads. It very likely still does — R3 is strong on both — so this is a cheap way to convert a one-sided check into a genuinely two-sided robustness claim.
findings.demo_risk: Presenting a one-directional sweep as 'the top risk survives the weight choice' invites the obvious question 'what about weighting secondary higher?' — the exact case the loss data suggests matters most for a PII-heavy SaaS. Easy to pre-empt.

findings.feature: Executive-facing headline numbers (exec summary vs dashboard)
findings.problem: Two shipped leadership artifacts headline different numbers for the same decision. The executive summary leads with the v1.0 (pre-research) figures — 65.7% -> 25.5%, 'R1 cuts by 11.7 points... nearly double R3's 6 points' — with no note that these were later revised. The dashboard headlines v1.1 — 56.7% -> 20.5%, R1 marginal 14.8pp vs R3 3.7pp. A leader reading only the memo gets the un-calibrated numbers as final.
findings.evidence: 07_executive_summary.md line 5: '65.7% today; ... reduces that to 25.5% ... phishing-resistant MFA against R1 cuts aggregate breach likelihood by 11.7 points on its own, nearly double R3's 6 points.' dashboard.html line 101: '56.7%' with '65.7%' struck through as prior; marg values R1 14.8 / R3 3.7 in dashboard vs 11.7 / 6.0 in the memo. README explains the v1.0/v1.1 split but the memo itself has no pointer.
findings.fix: Add one sentence at the top of 07 flagging that these are the as-run v1.0 figures and that a research-calibrated v1.1 (56.7% baseline) supersedes them (see dashboard/README). Or regenerate the memo's headline on v1.1. The marginal-contribution gap (R1 nearly-double vs R1 ~4x R3) should also be reconciled to one model version.
findings.demo_risk: If an evaluator opens the memo and the dashboard side by side, the mismatched headline breach probabilities and different 'R1 vs R3' multiples look like an un-reconciled artifact rather than a deliberate as-run/calibrated split — which the README actually handles well. The memo is the weak link.

findings.feature: Deterministic marginal_contribution labeling
findings.problem: The field labeled 'marginal_contribution' / 'delta_pp_vs_baseline' is actually the STANDALONE (first-in) effect of each mitigation applied alone against the all-baseline portfolio, not a true marginal (last-in) contribution. In a multiplicative 1-Prod(1-p) aggregate these differ, and the ordering conclusion ('R1 is the biggest lever') happens to survive, but the name promises marginal-in-context.
findings.evidence: advanced_analytics.py lines 116-120 replicate the workflow: `ps = [residual if j==i else baseline ...]` then `base - aggregate(ps)` — i.e. only risk i mitigated, all others at baseline. That is the standalone effect, labeled 'delta_pp_vs_baseline' and described in README as 'largest marginal effect'.
findings.fix: Either rename to 'standalone_reduction_if_only_this_mitigation' (the dashboard sub-label at line 154 already says this correctly) or additionally compute the true last-in marginal (full portfolio minus portfolio-without-i) and note they agree on ordering. Cheap precision win.
findings.demo_risk: Minor, but a FAIR-literate reviewer distinguishes standalone vs marginal contribution; using 'marginal' for the standalone number is a small vocabulary slip in an otherwise careful model.

strong_keep: Correlated Monte Carlo genuinely delivers its claimed ~0.40 pairwise rank correlation on baselines — verified empirically by replicating the exact draw order (measured mean pairwise Spearman 0.398 for CORR_W=0.4). The mixture-of-comonotonic construction (per-iteration Bernoulli(w) sharing one quantile shock across ALL risks) is mathematically correct, and the honest disclosure that v1 of the script only achieved ~w^2 and was fixed is a credibility asset, not a liability. Do not let any refactor break this.

The independence assumption is disclosed honestly, repeatedly, and with correct directional reasoning (correlation makes true baseline somewhat LOWER and residual somewhat HIGHER) in outputs/07, README, and ASSUMPTIONS.md M2. This is exactly the right treatment of a known-wrong simplifying assumption.

The adversarial TEF/VULN double-count challenge (outputs/03) is substantive and self-consistent: it uses the panel's own R4 VULN scores to bound R3's VULN, catches a real conditioning error (TEF counting delivery attempts while VULN also counts chain success), and is surfaced as a human-review flag rather than auto-resolved. This is the strongest reasoning artifact in the core.

All internal arithmetic is consistent and reproducible: per-risk residuals (baseline x (1-effectiveness) with correct low=b_low x (1-e_high) cross-pairing), the 1-Prod(1-p) aggregate, composites (L x I with L=(TEF+VULN)/2, I=0.6P+0.4S), ROSI, and payback all recompute exactly. Loss-band midpoints are consistent arithmetic midpoints with the open >$2M band explicitly disclosed as a $3M policy choice. The framework sensitivity correctly flags the R1/R2 0.1-gap near-tie.
data.harshest_single_criticism: The file that runs a 100,000-iteration Monte Carlo — and therefore has the distributional mean sitting right there — then computes 'Expected Annual Loss' on the MODE of an admittedly right-skewed triangular, understating the portfolio expected loss by ~13.6% (up to 18% on individual risks) while calling it 'Expected'. It is the one place where the quant core does something a numerate reviewer will read as not knowing that E[X] uses the mean, not the mode — in an otherwise unusually rigorous and self-aware analysis.
data.summary: The analytical core is well above the median take-home: the arithmetic is fully reproducible and internally consistent, the independence limitation and the v1.0->v1.1 recalibration are disclosed with correct directional reasoning, the correlated Monte Carlo actually delivers its claimed ~0.4 rank correlation (empirically verified), and the adversarial TEF/VULN double-count critique is genuinely sharp. The defects are calibration/labeling issues, not broken math: (1) 'Expected' Annual Loss is computed on the mode of a skewed distribution, biasing it ~14% low despite the mean being available — a real methodological/naming inconsistency and the highest-value fix; (2) the impact-weight sensitivity only sweeps toward primary-dominance (0.50->0.70), never testing the secondary-dominant view that R2/R3's own loss bands suggest matters most, so the 'top risk is stable' robustness claim is one-sided; (3) the executive summary headlines superseded v1.0 numbers (65.7%, R1 11.7pp) with no pointer to the calibrated v1.1 the dashboard uses (56.7%, R1 14.8pp), risking a coherence ding if memo and dashboard are read together; (4) 'marginal_contribution' is really the standalone/first-in effect, a minor vocabulary slip. None of these change the ranking or the fund-R1-and-R3 conclusion; all four are cheap to fix and would harden the most numerate part of the submission against exactly the questions a quant-literate evaluator will ask.

data.area: Security substance: the 5 risks (outputs/01), mitigation packages and named controls (outputs/04), IT-feasibility reviews, MITRE ATT&CK + SOC 2 + KRIs (ROADMAP.md), and threat/effectiveness claims (SOURCES.md, outputs/09).

findings.feature: MITRE ATT&CK mapping — R2 and R5 use T1213 (Data from Information Repositories) for a PostgreSQL customer-database dump
findings.problem: T1213 'Data from Information Repositories' is scoped by MITRE to collaboration/knowledge stores (SharePoint, Confluence, wikis, code repos), NOT a backend relational database that the application queries. Both R2 (SQLi/IDOR dumping the multi-tenant PostgreSQL) and R5 (direct contractor Postgres login exporting customer tables) map the customer-data-theft stage to T1213. For R2 the exfil is application/SQL-driven; for R5 it is a direct DB client session. Neither is an 'information repository' in ATT&CK's sense. The register's own R2 kill chain already has T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing App) which more naturally leads to collection from the DB — a database dump has no crisp single ATT&CK Collection technique, which is exactly why a sharp reviewer expects to see that acknowledged rather than force-fit to T1213.
findings.evidence: ROADMAP.md line 60: 'R2 | T1190 ... → T1213 Data from Information Repositories → T1048.002'; line 63: 'R5 | T1078.004 ... → T1213 Data from Information Repositories → T1048.002'. outputs/09 line 513 asserts 'All 22 technique mappings verified against live attack.mitre.org pages' and defends T1213 only for repo-secret harvesting, never re-examining the DB-dump usage.
findings.fix: For R2/R5 database exfiltration, drop T1213 and either (a) map collection to the app-layer technique already in-chain (data accessed via T1190 / valid DB session) noting ATT&CK lacks a precise 'dump SQL DB' Collection ID, or (b) if SharePoint/SSO-app bulk pull is meant, keep T1213 only where the target genuinely is SharePoint. Do not claim all 22 are 'verified' when the verification text never scrutinized this fit.
findings.demo_risk: A security architect who knows ATT&CK will spot T1213-for-a-SQL-dump immediately; combined with the bold 'all 22 verified against live pages' claim it reads as verification theater and undpercuts the whole research-calibration story.

findings.feature: SOC 2 criteria in the mitigation source-of-truth (outputs/04) were never corrected after the research audit dropped CC6.7 and CC9.2
findings.problem: The research pass (outputs/09 soc2_review) and the ROADMAP SOC 2 matrix explicitly DROP CC6.7 from R1 ('governs transmission/movement/removal, not authentication') and CC9.2 from R5 ('vendor/business-partner risk, not employee offboarding'). But outputs/04_mitigations.json — the artifact those tables are supposed to derive from — still lists CC6.7 in R1's soc2_criteria and CC9.2 in R5's. So the corrected mapping and the source mapping contradict each other. An auditor or evaluator who reads the actual mitigation JSON sees the un-corrected, wrong criteria.
findings.evidence: outputs/04_mitigations.json R1 soc2_criteria (lines 160-167) includes 'CC6.7' and 'CC7.3'; R5 soc2_criteria (lines 465-471) includes 'CC9.2'. Contrast ROADMAP.md line 75 '~~CC6.7~~ (dropped)' and line 81 '~~CC9.2~~ (dropped)'.
findings.fix: Regenerate outputs/04's soc2_criteria arrays from the audited set (remove CC6.7/CC7.3 from R1, remove CC9.2 from R5, add A1.3 to R3, CC6.3 to R2) so the JSON, ROADMAP, and outputs/09 all agree — or add one line to ROADMAP noting outputs/04 holds the pre-audit set.
findings.demo_risk: The submission's central selling point is auditability. A reviewer who diffs the corrected matrix against its own source JSON finds them disagreeing on the exact criteria — the SOC 2 mapping is the most auditor-facing artifact, so this drift lands where it hurts most.

findings.feature: R1 identity package omits the OAuth consent / device-code bypass controls that its own research says defeat phishing-resistant MFA
findings.problem: The R1 effectiveness evidence (outputs/09 line 67, SOURCES.md line 23) states plainly that Storm-2372 device-code phishing and OAuth consent phishing 'succeed WITHOUT touching the authentication-strength control' and that the mode could only rise to ~85% 'if the rollout also disables the OAuth device-code flow and restricts user app consent in Conditional Access.' Those two concrete, free, E3-native controls (a CA policy blocking the device-code authentication flow; restricting user consent to verified/low-risk apps in Entra) are named nowhere in the R1 control set or quick wins. The package claims to 'remove the phishable factor' but leaves the two documented bypass channels open.
findings.evidence: outputs/04_mitigations.json R1 controls (lines 111-141) cover passkeys, CA session hardening, ID Protection, runbook, phishing sims — no device-code-flow block, no app-consent restriction. residual_gaps (line 169) mentions token theft but not device-code/consent. outputs/09 line 71 explicitly names both as the missing bypass closures.
findings.fix: Add two quick wins to R1: (1) CA policy blocking the OAuth device authorization grant flow for users who don't need it; (2) set user app-consent to 'allow for verified publishers / low-impact permissions only' with an admin-consent workflow. Both are $0 on E3 and directly close the Storm-2372/consent-phishing gap the research flags.
findings.demo_risk: An evaluator who reads outputs/09 then the R1 controls sees the pipeline diagnosed the exact bypass and then didn't prescribe the fix — the kind of gap a vCISO reviewer will call out as 'you knew and still left it open.'

findings.feature: Aggregate baseline probability headline (65.7% v1.0 / 56.7% v1.1) still exceeds the ceiling the model's own research supports
findings.problem: The joint-probability research finding (outputs/09 line 53) concludes a defensible P(>=1 material >$25k incident/yr) is 'roughly 15-35%, not ~66%,' and recommends recalibrating to ~25-40%. v1.1 only came down to 56.7% (SOURCES.md line 57) — still above the recommended band — because it applied the lower per-risk modes but did not model the shared-root-cause correlation the research says is the second structural bias (R1/R3/R4 all share weak identity + tiny IT team). The honest thing (documented in outputs/09) was not carried into the headline the exec summary quotes to 3 sig figs.
findings.evidence: outputs/09 line 53 suggested_change: 'Recalibrate so the joint probability ... lands ~25-40%'; SOURCES.md line 57 shows v1.1 aggregate baseline 56.7%, still above 40%. SOURCES.md line 66 admits 'Survey-based figures measure orgs experiencing >=1 event, not calibrated per-org probabilities.'
findings.fix: Either apply the correlation adjustment (Gaussian copula or shared Bernoulli across identity-rooted risks) to bring the aggregate into the research-supported 25-40% band, or print the caveat inline next to every headline use of 56.7% that it sits above the model's own recommended ceiling and why.
findings.demo_risk: Leadership will anchor on 'a two-in-three (or better-than-even) chance of a breach this year.' If the number is above what the cited claims data supports and the submission knows it, a sharp reader treats the headline as fear-inflated — damaging for a risk-quantification deliverable whose whole value is calibration.

findings.feature: Right-sizing for a 3-person team is genuinely real, not lip service — feasibility reviews materially rewrote every package
findings.problem: No defect. The IT-feasibility layer is the strongest security-substance element: it caught the alert-fatigue reality (Defender noise to a team with no rota becomes 'wallpaper within a month'), the manual employeeLeaveDateTime failure mode that re-introduces the very dormant-account risk R5 claims to kill, the Postgres-network-lockdown-vs-contractor-access collision between R3 and R5, the RLS-under-PgBouncer connection-pooling break, and the Defender-XDR-attack-disruption dependency on Defender for Endpoint/Cloud Apps not yet deployed. These are the exact objections a working IT manager raises.
findings.evidence: outputs/04 line 85 (Defender noise → wallpaper), line 491 (employeeLeaveDateTime manual failure re-creates dormant account), line 84 + line 292 (Postgres lockdown collides with contractor DB access), line 286 (RLS + PgBouncer transaction pooling), line 186 (XDR disruption depends on undeployed sensors).
findings.fix: Keep as-is. Do not let any fix pass flatten these feasibility concerns into clean 'approved' packages — the pushback is the credibility.
findings.demo_risk: This is what separates the submission from the '200 mediocre' pile; preserve it.

findings.feature: Control selection is native, current, and correctly discounted — OIDC federation, passkeys/FIDO2 auth strengths, Defender for Business, Entra auth on Postgres, immutable WORM + MUA vault backups
findings.problem: No defect. The named Azure/M365 controls are the right current-generation choices: GitHub Actions OIDC workload identity federation to kill long-lived SP secrets (correct primary control for R3), phishing-resistant auth strengths + passkeys (correct root-cause fix for AiTM, not generic MFA), Defender for Business as the sub-300-seat EDR SKU (correctly noting E3's Defender P1 has no EDR/AIR), immutable Blob WORM + Azure Backup vault with multi-user authorization so a Contributor-level attacker can't destroy recovery data, and Entra-auth-on-Postgres to collapse standing contractor logins. Effectiveness ranges are also correctly capped against real bypass evidence (WAFFLED WAF bypasses, Picus 62% prevention / 14% alerting).
findings.evidence: outputs/04 line 8 (OIDC primary control), line 115 (passkeys via CA auth strengths), line 335 (Defender for Business, E3 P1 has no EDR), line 37 (immutable WORM + MUA vault), line 419 (Entra auth on Postgres single revocation point); outputs/09 line 76 (WAF bypass discount), line 94 (Picus EDR discount).
findings.fix: Keep. These are defensible to a vCISO and correctly scoped for the team size and licensing.
findings.demo_risk: Strong technical core; don't regress the effectiveness discounts into round optimistic numbers.

findings.feature: R4 ATT&CK chain ordering lists privilege escalation (T1548.002 Bypass UAC) after credential theft, and Bypass-UAC is a weak fit for a local-admin fleet
findings.problem: Two small precision issues. (1) The R4 register scenario says users are already local admins and the loader 'escalates via local admin'; T1548.002 Bypass User Account Control is about circumventing UAC to elevate WITHOUT admin consent — for an already-local-admin user it is largely moot; T1548 (parent) or a token/OS credential technique fits the stated scenario better. (2) In outputs/09 the R4 technique array orders T1555.003 (browser credential theft) before T1548.002 (UAC bypass), i.e. credential access before privilege escalation, which inverts the usual chain and the register's own narrative (escalate, then steal tokens). ROADMAP line 62 silently reorders them again. Minor but it is exactly the kind of thing the 'verified kill chain' claim invites scrutiny of.
findings.evidence: outputs/01 line 33 'escalates via local admin, steals tokens'; outputs/09 lines 434-451 order T1204.002 → T1555.003 → T1548.002 → T1528; ROADMAP.md line 62 orders T1204.002 → T1548.002 → T1555.003 + T1528.
findings.fix: For a local-admin fleet, drop or downgrade T1548.002 (or replace with T1078 abuse of existing admin rights) and make the credential-access-vs-escalation ordering consistent across outputs/09 and ROADMAP with the register narrative.
findings.demo_risk: Low individually, but paired with the T1213 issue it lets a reviewer argue the ATT&CK layer was pattern-matched, not reasoned — cheap to fix, so worth fixing.

findings.feature: Coverage gaps are named but two of the three (backup integrity, third-party SaaS/vendor breach) are not carried into any scored risk or KRI
findings.problem: The register's coverage_gaps section honestly flags backup integrity/recoverability, third-party SaaS/vendor breach, and current-insider overexposure as uncovered. Backup integrity is partly picked up by R3's immutable-backup control, but third-party SaaS/vendor breach — a real top-tier risk for a company reselling through ~15 SSO apps and data processors — gets no risk entry, no mitigation, no KRI, and no SOC 2 vendor-risk control (ironically CC9.2, which was dropped from R5, IS the right home for it). Naming a gap and then giving it zero treatment or even a placeholder KRI is weaker than a one-line 'R6 candidate: vendor risk / TPRM' note.
findings.evidence: outputs/01 lines 52-54 name all three gaps; no KRI in ROADMAP table (lines 40-51) covers vendor/third-party risk; CC9.2 was dropped entirely (ROADMAP line 81) rather than repurposed for vendor risk.
findings.fix: Add a short 'R6 (out-of-scope-for-depth) — third-party/vendor breach' stub with one KRI (e.g. % of the 15 SSO vendors with a current SOC 2 report on file) and anchor CC9.2 there, converting a named gap into a tracked one.
findings.demo_risk: For a B2B SaaS, an evaluator expects TPRM to at least appear; flagging it then dropping the one criterion that fits reads as an oversight rather than a scoping decision.

strong_keep: The IT-feasibility layer's concrete, team-sized objections — Defender alert fatigue with no rota, the manual employeeLeaveDateTime failure that re-creates R5's dormant-account risk, the R3-Postgres-lockdown vs R5-contractor-access collision, RLS breaking under PgBouncer transaction pooling, and Defender-XDR-disruption depending on undeployed sensors

Correct, current native control choices with honest effectiveness discounts: GitHub Actions OIDC federation as the R3 primary control, phishing-resistant auth strengths + passkeys (not generic MFA) for AiTM, Defender for Business as the sub-300-seat EDR SKU noting E3 P1 lacks EDR/AIR, immutable WORM + multi-user-auth backup vault, Entra-auth-on-Postgres as the single revocation point

The effectiveness ceilings that are capped against real bypass evidence (WAFFLED WAF parsing bypasses → WAF alone can't carry 80%; Picus 62% prevention / only 14% alerting → EDR high mode capped for a team with no triage)

The risk-register validator stripping unsupported invented facts (no-secret-scanning, single high-priv DB role, shared service accounts) and demoting them to confirm-in-fieldwork notes, which keeps the scored risks honest to the actual company profile
data.harshest_single_criticism: The submission's headline credibility rests on 'verified' framework mappings and self-critical calibration, yet the two most auditor-facing security artifacts quietly contradict their own verification: outputs/04's SOC 2 criteria still carry CC6.7 (R1) and CC9.2 (R5) that the research pass explicitly dropped, and the ATT&CK layer force-fits T1213 'Data from Information Repositories' onto a PostgreSQL SQL-injection/direct-login database dump for R2 and R5 while claiming all 22 techniques were verified against live MITRE pages. A sharp evaluator who trusts the 'auditable, not a black box' claim and then diffs the corrected matrix against its source JSON, or checks the T1213 scope, finds the verification is not as tight as advertised — which is more damaging than an honestly-flagged gap because it turns the submission's biggest strength (rigor) into its exposure.
data.summary: The security substance is genuinely strong and above the mediocre baseline: the five risks are well-differentiated with explicit distinctness boundaries, the mitigation packages name correct, current, right-sized Azure/M365 controls (OIDC federation, passkeys via auth strengths, Defender for Business, immutable/MUA backups, Entra-auth-on-Postgres), effectiveness is honestly discounted against real bypass literature, and the IT-feasibility reviews deliver the exact team-capacity and cross-risk-collision objections a real IT manager raises — the right-sizing is real, not lip service. The defects are precision-and-consistency issues concentrated in the framework mappings, and they matter disproportionately because auditability is the pitch: (1) the ATT&CK T1213 misfit for SQL-database exfil in R2/R5 under an 'all 22 verified' banner; (2) outputs/04 still lists CC6.7/CC9.2 that the audit dropped, contradicting the corrected ROADMAP matrix; (3) the R1 package diagnoses the OAuth-consent/device-code bypass in its own research but never prescribes the two free CA controls that close it; (4) the aggregate baseline (56.7%) still sits above the 25-40% ceiling the model's own claims research supports. None is dangerous advice; all are cheap to fix. Fix the mapping/consistency drift and add the two missing CA quick wins, and the security core is hire-grade — but do not let a cleanup pass flatten the feasibility pushback or the effectiveness discounts, which are the credibility.

data.area: Engineering / plumbing: orchestration (risk_pipeline.workflow.js), replay harness, schemas.json, advanced_analytics.py, dashboard.html

findings.feature: Replay harness "byte-identical PASS" claim
findings.problem: The headline reproducibility claim is real, not trivial-by-construction. I mutation-tested it: changing the impact weight in the workflow from 0.6/0.4 to 0.7/0.3 makes the harness print FAIL (mismatches in scoring.ranking, scoring.table) and exit 1. So the harness genuinely re-executes the deterministic code paths (median aggregation, L*I composite, ranking, tie-breaks, aggregate P(breach), marginals) and would catch a regression. It is NOT just diffing a file against itself.
findings.evidence: Mutation test: replacing '0.6 * med.primary_impact + 0.4 * med.secondary_impact' -> 0.7/0.3 yields 'FAIL — mismatches in: scoring.ranking, scoring.table'. Clean run yields 'PASS ... byte-identical'.
findings.fix: Keep as-is. Do not let a later 'fix' collapse this into a self-comparison. One cheap upgrade: bundle the mutation test as an assertion so reviewers see the harness can fail.
findings.demo_risk: A sharp evaluator who suspects the PASS is circular will try exactly this mutation; it holds up, which is a credibility win. Leave it intact.

findings.feature: Correlated Monte Carlo variant (rank-correlation claim)
findings.problem: The comment/output claims the correlated variant delivers pairwise rank correlation ~= w = 0.4, and states a prior version only achieved ~w^2 (the 'assessor-caught bug'). I reproduced the shared-shock draw logic and measured empirical Spearman rank correlation at 0.404 and 0.399 for two risk pairs — the claim is quantitatively accurate, and the mixture-of-comonotonic construction is a correct way to hit a target rank correlation.
findings.evidence: Independent 50k-iteration reproduction: 'spearman(0,1): 0.404', 'spearman(2,3): 0.399' against the code's stated '~= 0.4'.
findings.fix: Keep. This is the single most technically credible piece of the analytics — a fix pass must not touch the shared-shock logic or the CORR_W plumbing.
findings.demo_risk: This is the kind of detail that separates a strong submission; the narrative ('v1 had ~w^2, fixed to ~w') is backed by working, verifiable code.

findings.feature: Monte Carlo seeding / RNG stream shared across the two parameter sets
findings.problem: A single Random(SEED) is created once (line 273) and threaded through monte_carlo() for v1.0 first, then v1.1. Because v1.0 consumes a variable number of draws before v1.1 runs, v1.1's entire 100k-iteration stream starts wherever v1.0 happened to leave the generator. The output is deterministic for the fixed loop order, so today's file is reproducible — but the two versions are not drawn from independent, self-contained streams, and simply reordering the versions tuple (or adding a risk to v1.0) silently changes every v1.1 Monte Carlo number. For a script whose entire selling point is 'seeded, deterministic, reproducible', this is a latent reproducibility footgun and slightly undercuts the independence framing.
findings.evidence: Line 273 'rng = random.Random(SEED)' then line 275 'for name, params in (("v1.0_pipeline", v10), ("v1.1_research_calibrated", V11))' — the same rng passed to monte_carlo(params, rng) for both. Demonstrated stream-position sensitivity: first-5 draws after 1M consumed draws are completely different from a fresh seed.
findings.fix: Re-seed per (version, analysis) with a derived seed, e.g. pass random.Random(SEED + hash(name) % 1000) or reset rng.seed(SEED) at the top of each version's monte_carlo call. Cheap, makes each block independently reproducible, and matches the reproducibility claim.
findings.demo_risk: A staff engineer reading advanced_analytics.py will spot the shared rng in ~10 seconds and ask 'why is v1.1 conditioned on v1.0's draw count?' It doesn't break today's output but it reads as a determinism blind spot in the one file that advertises determinism.

findings.feature: Replay harness regex that strips 'export const meta'
findings.problem: The harness loads the workflow as text and evals the body after stripping the meta block with the regex /^export const meta[\s\S]*?\n\}\n/. This is fragile string surgery: it assumes meta is the first line and terminates at the first occurrence of newline-brace-newline. The meta object contains nested arrays of objects (phases); the non-greedy match happens to stop at the correct closing brace today only because of exact formatting. Reindent meta, add a trailing property after the phases array, or convert to a different brace layout and the strip either removes too little (leaving 'export' -> SyntaxError) or too much (eating pipeline code). The whole 'reproducibility is provable' story then breaks on a cosmetic edit to an unrelated block.
findings.evidence: Line 63 'body = body.replace(/^export const meta[\s\S]*?\n\}\n/, \'\')' then line 65 constructs an AsyncFunction from the remaining text. Confirmed it works today ('meta present after strip: false'), but it is coupled to exact source formatting.
findings.fix: Make the workflow export its runnable body as a named async function (or move meta to a separate module) so the harness imports it instead of regex-stripping-and-eval'ing source. If that's too invasive for the demo, at least assert after the strip that the result no longer contains 'export const meta' AND still contains a known first token, and throw a clear error otherwise.
findings.demo_risk: Eval-of-regex-stripped-source is exactly the pattern a reviewer flags as 'clever but brittle.' It works, but it makes the otherwise-solid harness look hacky and one refactor away from silently breaking.

findings.feature: Schema duplication between schemas.json and the workflow file
findings.problem: The seven JSON Schemas exist twice: as live JS objects inside risk_pipeline.workflow.js (the ones actually passed to agents) and as a hand-maintained copy in schemas/schemas.json presented as 'the pipeline's tool definitions.' They are not imported from a single source, so they can drift. They are already inconsistent in structure: schemas.json models REVISED_QUANT_SCHEMA with bespoke non-standard keys ('extends','additional_required','additional_properties') while the workflow spells out the full required list and merges properties via Object.assign. A reviewer diffing the two will find the JSON copy is a lossy paraphrase, not the real contract.
findings.evidence: schemas.json:154-162 uses '"extends": "QUANT_SCHEMA", "additional_required": [...], "additional_properties": {...}' — invalid JSON-Schema keywords — whereas workflow:226-232 uses a real merged schema. The _comment at schemas.json:2 calls these 'the pipeline's tool definitions', implying authority they don't have.
findings.fix: Either (a) delete schemas/schemas.json and point the README at the schemas in the workflow file as the single source of truth, or (b) have the workflow import the schemas from a shared .json/.mjs and generate schemas.json from it. Cheapest for a demo: (a) plus a one-line note 'schemas are defined inline in the workflow; reproduced here for readability.'
findings.demo_risk: Low-stakes, but a careful reviewer who notices two copies will wonder which one ran, and the invalid 'extends/additional_*' keys in the JSON copy read as 'author didn't actually validate this file.'

findings.feature: Replay coverage — what the harness does NOT verify
findings.problem: README (line 87, 211) says the harness re-runs 'the exact pipeline script' and byte-diffs 'the results,' which reads as full coverage. In fact the eq() diffs cover only scoring.ranking, scoring.table, quant model, final_estimates, and top-risk verdict. It does NOT verify the assembled human_review_flags (outputs/06), the executive summary (07 is fed in and never diffed — correct, since it's LLM text), or advanced_analytics (08). The determinism story is real but narrower than the prose implies; 06's flag-assembly logic (workflow lines 575-600) runs but its output is never checked against the shipped 06 file.
findings.evidence: Only six eq() calls at replay_harness.mjs:73-78. No reference to '06' or 'human_review_flags' anywhere in the harness (grep returned nothing). README:211 'byte-diffs the results — the plumbing is provable.'
findings.fix: Either add an eq('human_review_flags', result.human_review_flags, load('06_human_review_flags.json')) assertion (the flag-assembly is deterministic and would strengthen the claim), or soften the README to 'byte-diffs the ranking, scoring table and quant model' so the claim matches the six checks.
findings.demo_risk: Minor, but the gap between 'the plumbing is provable' and 'we check 6 of the deterministic outputs' is the kind of thing a skeptical evaluator will call an overclaim if they read the harness closely.

findings.feature: advanced_analytics determinism (byte-identical reruns)
findings.problem: Ran the script twice: the regenerated outputs/08_advanced_analytics.json is byte-identical to the committed file and byte-identical run-to-run. json.dump with fixed indent, sorted percentile helper, and a fixed seed make the artifact genuinely reproducible on this machine. The _provenance string ('No LLM produced any number in this file') is accurate — every number traces to arithmetic over the JSON artifacts.
findings.evidence: diff of committed 08 vs regenerated: 'MATCHES committed'; two consecutive runs: 'PY RUN1==RUN2 byte-identical'. Dashboard headline numbers (56.7%->20.5%, ROSI 221%, portfolio EAL $1.687M->$0.526M, P(resid<=35%)=93%, tornado R2 baseline 10.1) all reproduced exactly from the freshly generated 08.
findings.fix: Keep. (Combine with the RNG-reseeding fix above so it stays reproducible under version reordering.)
findings.demo_risk: Positive signal — the numbers on the dashboard survive a from-scratch recompute, which is exactly what an evaluator will spot-check.

findings.feature: Dashboard self-containment and provenance honesty
findings.problem: The dashboard is a single self-contained HTML fragment: inline CSS, inline vanilla-JS SVG rendering, zero external requests, no CDN/fonts/images — CSP-safe by construction. Data is hardcoded from the artifacts with an explicit provenance comment mapping each block to its source file, and it is honest that it is 'hand-built from the JSON artifacts' rather than pretending to be a live read. Every spot-checked number (ranking, ties, marginals, tornado, ROSI, EAL) matches outputs/02 and outputs/08.
findings.evidence: dashboard.html:1-5 provenance comment; data literals at 203-228 with '// v1.1 research-calibrated parameters (outputs/08...)'; verified R1<->R2 composite gap = 0.1 (17.6/15.3/15.2 in outputs/02) matches the 'tied' chip; verified v1.1 marginals [R1 14.8, R2 3.9, R3 3.7, R4 5.0, R5 1.6] match the dashboard array exactly.
findings.fix: Keep. Only nit: the data is duplicated by hand from 08, so it can drift if 08 is regenerated with new numbers — worth a one-line comment noting the values are a manual snapshot, but not worth wiring up a build step for a demo.
findings.demo_risk: Positive — self-contained + honest-about-being-hand-built is the right call for an Artifact; don't let a fix pass try to make it 'live-load' the JSON (CSP would block it anyway).

findings.feature: Error-swallowing in parallel()/pipeline() shims and retry wrapper
findings.problem: parallel() and pipeline() swallow any thrown error and substitute null ('catch { return null }'), and the downstream code .filter(Boolean)s the nulls. In the real pipeline this is a reasonable 'one lost agent shouldn't kill the deliverable' choice, but in the replay harness it means a resolve() miss (e.g. a label the map doesn't cover) inside a parallel/pipeline stage would be silently dropped rather than surfaced — the run could quietly produce a short array and still PASS the diffs if the missing item didn't affect the six checked fields. The single-retry ask() wrapper (append '(Retry...)' and call once more) is also effectively dead in replay since the mock never returns null.
findings.evidence: replay_harness.mjs:51 'const parallel = (thunks) => Promise.all(thunks.map(async (t) => { try { return await t(); } catch { return null; } }))'; :55 'try { ... } catch { return null; }'. resolve() throws on unknown labels (:45) but only outside parallel/pipeline stages does that throw propagate.
findings.fix: In the harness only, make the shims rethrow (or collect and report) instead of returning null, so a resolve() gap becomes a visible failure rather than a silently shorter array. The production ask()/retry logic can stay as-is; it's just untested by replay.
findings.demo_risk: Low. But if asked 'what happens if an agent output is missing?', the honest answer is 'the harness would hide it' — worth tightening so the reproducibility proof can't pass on a partial run.

strong_keep: Replay harness is a real reproducibility check, not circular — mutation-testing the impact-weight formula flips it to FAIL/exit 1; it genuinely re-executes median aggregation, L*I composite, ranking, aggregate P(breach) and marginals against the shipped artifacts.

Correlated Monte Carlo variant is correct and its headline claim is verifiable: measured Spearman rank correlation 0.40 matches the stated CORR_W=0.4, and the 'fixed from ~w^2' narrative is backed by working code.

advanced_analytics.py is byte-identically reproducible run-to-run and matches the committed 08; the 'no LLM produced any number here' provenance is accurate and every dashboard figure recomputes exactly.

dashboard.html is a genuinely self-contained, CSP-safe single fragment (inline CSS+JS SVG, zero external requests) that is honest about being hand-built from the JSON artifacts, with a per-block provenance comment.
data.harshest_single_criticism: The two files whose entire pitch is 'deterministic and reproducible' each carry a determinism blind spot that works today only by luck of formatting/ordering: advanced_analytics.py threads one RNG through both parameter sets so v1.1's Monte Carlo is silently conditioned on how many draws v1.0 consumed (reorder the versions tuple and every v1.1 number changes), and replay_harness.mjs runs the workflow by regex-stripping 'export const meta' out of the source text and eval'ing the rest — a cosmetic reformat of the unrelated meta block breaks the whole reproducibility proof. Neither is wrong output-wise right now, but both are exactly the fragility a staff engineer is trained to spot in code that advertises rigor.
data.summary: The engineering is well above the median take-home: the replay harness is a real (mutation-provable) reproducibility check rather than a self-comparison, the analytics are byte-identically reproducible, the correlated-Monte-Carlo rank-correlation claim is empirically true (0.40 measured vs 0.4 claimed), and the dashboard is a clean, honest, self-contained artifact whose every number recomputes from the JSON. Nothing here is broken or misleading in its output. The defects are craft-level fragilities and small honesty gaps, not wrong results: (1) a single shared RNG makes v1.1's Monte Carlo order-dependent on v1.0 — reseed per version; (2) the harness eval's the workflow after a formatting-fragile regex meta-strip — export a runnable function or assert post-strip; (3) schemas exist in two places (workflow JS + a lossy, invalid-keyword JSON copy) that can drift — pick one source of truth; (4) the README says the harness byte-diffs 'the results' but it only checks six deterministic fields (06 flag-assembly and 08 are not verified) — either add the 06 assertion or narrow the prose; (5) harness shims swallow errors to null, so a partial replay could still PASS. Fixing the RNG reseed and the meta-strip are the two that would most protect the candidate in front of a sharp reviewer; the strong pieces (mutation-proof replay, verified correlation model, reproducible analytics, self-contained honest dashboard) must survive any fix pass untouched.

data.area: Writing & demo-readiness (README, executive summary, ASSUMPTIONS, SOURCES, HUMAN_REVIEW_FLAGS)

findings.feature: Executive summary — flag count claim
findings.problem: The exec summary's 'Where not to trust this report' section states the report 'carries 23 open human-review flags' with no mention of the research pass. But every other document (this file's own line-1 editorial note, README lines 196–204, HUMAN_REVIEW_FLAGS.md post-research status table) says the v1.1 research pass WORKED/closed most of those flags, leaving only ~2 genuinely open (notification-duty materiality; 3-person-team under-delivery). The exec summary body is stale v1.0 text bolted under a v1.1 header, and it directly contradicts the rest of the submission on the single most reputation-sensitive number in the doc — how much you should distrust it.
findings.evidence: Line 39: 'This report carries 23 open human-review flags, clustering around a few themes...' vs HUMAN_REVIEW_FLAGS.md line 9+ table marking themes 'Worked' and only two 'Open', and exec summary line 1: 'The ranking and all qualitative conclusions are unchanged.'
findings.fix: Change '23 open' to '23 flags raised by the run, of which 2 remain genuinely open after the research pass (notification-duty materiality; concurrent-delivery capacity)'. One sentence reconciles it with the header and the other docs.
findings.demo_risk: A sharp evaluator who read README first and then the exec summary sees the two artifacts disagree on the headline trust caveat. Reads as copy-paste that was never reconciled — the exact 'author-not-reader' sloppiness that undercuts a submission whose whole pitch is rigor.

findings.feature: Second-pass agent count
findings.problem: The intro (line 5) calls the research pass a '7-agent pass'; README lines 172, 210, 228 and all of SOURCES.md call it '6 web-research verifiers'. HUMAN_REVIEW_FLAGS.md line 9 reconciles it as '6 verifiers + an assessor' (=7), so it is technically defensible, but the reader is handed 7 and 6 with no bridge and will read it as an uncounted/miscounted number in a doc that leans hard on precise counts (24 agents, 23 flags, 28 quick wins).
findings.evidence: Line 5: 'A second 7-agent pass then web-verified every parameter' vs line 172: 'a second agentic pass (6 web-research verifiers...)'.
findings.fix: Pick one framing everywhere: either '7-agent pass (6 verifiers + 1 assessor)' at first mention, or say '6 verifiers' consistently. One-word edit at line 5.
findings.demo_risk: On a 10-minute skim the evaluator spots two different counts for the same thing on the same page. Small, but in a rigor-first submission it plants doubt about whether the bigger numbers were checked.

findings.feature: Marginal-contribution table rounding
findings.problem: The README v1.0 marginal table and the exec summary both quote R1 = -11.7 pp, R2 = -5.5 pp, R5 = -2.1 pp, but the source JSON (outputs/08_advanced_analytics.json, v1.0_pipeline.marginal_contribution) has R1 = 11.8, R2 = 5.6, R5 = 2.2. The prose systematically rounds down by 0.1 across three rows including the headline 'largest single lever' number. The R3=6.0 and R4=3.9 rows match, so it is not a consistent rounding rule — it looks like the numbers were hand-transcribed and drifted from the artifact they claim to be recomputable from.
findings.evidence: README line 160 'R1 ... -11.7 pp'; JSON marginal_contribution R1 delta_pp_vs_baseline = 11.8. Same 0.1 gap on R2 (5.5 vs 5.6) and R5 (2.1 vs 2.2).
findings.fix: Regenerate the table straight from the JSON (11.8/5.6/2.2) or state a rounding convention. The README explicitly claims 'You can recompute every number in this report by hand from the JSON' — so any mismatch is self-inflicted.
findings.demo_risk: The submission's core credibility claim is 'all arithmetic by code, numbers traceable to JSON.' An evaluator who spot-checks the headline lever (11.7 vs 11.8 in the file) catches the prose disagreeing with its own artifact — the worst possible place to be sloppy given the pitch.

findings.feature: Dual-model (v1.0 vs v1.1) story clarity
findings.problem: The dual-model story is defensible in principle (as-run artifact preserved, research-calibrated model shown separately) but the reader is forced to hold two full sets of headline numbers in their head simultaneously. Task 4 leads with the entire v1.0 model (65.7%→25.5%, -40.2pp, R1 -11.7) as 'the headline number,' then a later subsection swaps in v1.1 (56.7%→20.5%, -36.2pp, R1 -14.8). The exec summary is entirely v1.0 with a v1.1 note stapled on top. A skimming evaluator cannot answer 'so what is the actual breach-reduction number you stand behind?' without reading both sections and doing the reconciliation themselves.
findings.evidence: README line 149 '(the headline number...)' presents 65.7%→25.5%; line 177 table then presents v1.1 56.7%→20.5% as the calibrated answer; exec summary line 5 quotes 65.7%→25.5% as the live figure with only a top-of-file caveat.
findings.fix: State the answer once, up front, in v1.1 terms ('~57% today → ~21% after mitigation') and treat v1.0 as an audit-trail appendix, not co-equal headline. Cut the v1.0 numbers out of the exec summary body entirely and quote only v1.1.
findings.demo_risk: The single most-asked question in the room ('how much safer does this make them?') has two answers in the doc. The candidate looks like they couldn't decide which model to commit to, when the honest-calibration story is actually a strength if told once and cleanly.

findings.feature: README length / density
findings.problem: The README is 234 lines and does not know when to stop. 'Beyond the brief' (11 bullets, lines 208–219), 'Pipeline changelog' (4 items, 221–228), and 'Reproducing / porting' (230–234) are all after the substantive content and largely restate features already covered in Tasks 2–4. The value-per-line falls off a cliff after the caveats section. A 10-minute evaluator is exhausted before reaching the genuinely strong replay-harness and framework-sensitivity material because it is buried at the same visual weight as gold-plating.
findings.evidence: Lines 208–219 'Beyond the brief — features that make this operationally useful' restates research-verification, replay harness, Monte Carlo, ROSI, roadmap, MITRE, SOC 2, marginal-contribution — each already described earlier in the document.
findings.fix: Cut 'Beyond the brief' to a 4-line bullet list of extras with links, delete the porting section or move it to a CONTRIBUTING note, and fold the changelog into one line. Roughly 30% shorter reads as more confident, not less complete.
findings.demo_risk: Volume-as-signal is exactly what a jaded evaluator ('seen 200 mediocre submissions') penalizes. The length invites the thought 'this person can't distinguish their strong work from their padding.'

findings.feature: Opening two paragraphs / entry point
findings.problem: The single most important paragraph (line 5) front-loads process boasts — '24 agents', 'schema-validated JSON', 'all arithmetic by deterministic code', 'actually executed', 'second 7-agent pass web-verified every parameter' — before the reader knows the answer. The lede (R3 is the top risk; ~57%→~21% breach reduction; fund R1+R3 first) is buried at line 93+ and in a separate exec summary file. There is no single 'read this first' pointer at the top of the README to the 60-second story.
findings.evidence: Line 5 is entirely about how the pipeline works; the actual finding ('The single highest risk: R3') does not appear until line 101, and the fund-both recommendation until line 103.
findings.fix: Add a 3-line 'Bottom line' box at the very top: top risk R3, biggest lever R1, ~57%→~21% breach probability, fund R1+R3 now — then let the process story follow. Point explicitly to outputs/07 as the 1-page exec read.
findings.demo_risk: An evaluator skimming top-down hits a wall of methodology before any result. The work answers the business question well, but the writing makes them dig for it — the opposite of an executive-ready deliverable for 'leadership of a company like Meridian Desk.'

findings.feature: 'web-verified every parameter against current primary sources'
findings.problem: 'web-verified every parameter against current primary sources' overstates what SOURCES.md actually delivers. SOURCES.md itself says (line 66) the citations 'were gathered by AI research agents; URLs and figures were spot-checked for coherence but a human should click through the ~30 sources before board use,' and R5 effectiveness 'remains expert judgment' with no literature. So 'verified' is really 'AI-gathered and coherence-checked, not human-verified,' and at least one parameter was explicitly NOT verifiable against sources.
findings.evidence: README line 5 'web-verified every parameter against current primary sources' vs SOURCES.md line 66 'these citations were gathered by AI research agents... a human should click through' and line 27 R5 '⚠️ expert judgment (no quantitative literature exists)'.
findings.fix: Soften to 'AI-researched every parameter against current primary sources (human click-through still recommended; one parameter has no literature and stays labeled expert judgment)'. The honest version is still impressive and matches SOURCES.md.
findings.demo_risk: The claim in the intro is bolder than the caveat the candidate themselves wrote three files deep. An evaluator who reads both catches the intro overselling — undermines the 'honest about limits' brand the rest of the submission earns.

strong_keep: The intellectual honesty of the calibration story is genuinely strong: R5's baseline where the internal red team pushed UP (8→10%) and external research pushed back DOWN (→6%), both moves preserved in the audit trail (SOURCES.md line 17). This 'two independent checks converged' narrative is the best thing in the submission — do not flatten it when fixing the dual-model confusion.

The 'priorities are not sequencing' insight — R3 is top-RANKED (impact) but R1 is the biggest LIKELIHOOD lever (marginal-contribution table) — is a real, non-obvious analytical result surfaced by the pipeline, not decoration. Keep it prominent.

Numbers are overwhelmingly traceable: composites (17.6/15.3/15.2/12.6/11.9), aggregate baseline/residual (65.7/25.5, 56.7/20.5), ROSI (221%, R3 424%, R5 13%), and framework-sensitivity (R3 stable, R1/R2 swap at ≤0.55) all match the JSON exactly. Only the marginal-contribution 0.1 rounding drifts. The replay-harness + byte-identical claim is the credibility anchor — protect it.

HUMAN_REVIEW_FLAGS.md's post-research status table (worked vs. still-open, with the two genuinely-open items named) is exactly the mature 'where not to trust the AI' artifact the brief asks for. It is the model the stale exec-summary flag paragraph should be reconciled TO, not away from.

The red-team correlation/independence critique (shared infostealer node across R3/R4, shared identity weakness across R1/R3/R5, correlated under-delivery by one 3-person team) is sophisticated and honestly surfaced rather than hidden — a strong signal that the multi-agent design produces real dissent.
data.harshest_single_criticism: The executive summary — the one file a busy leader actually reads — is stale v1.0 text wearing a v1.1 hat: it quotes 65.7%→25.5% as the live number and declares '23 open human-review flags' in direct contradiction of the header on its own line 1 and every other document, which all say the research pass closed most of those flags and only 2 remain open. For a submission whose entire competitive edge is 'rigor and honest self-assessment,' having your executive one-pager contradict your own trust-caveat is the first thing a sharp evaluator rolls their eyes at.
data.summary: The analytical substance is strong and the numbers are ~95% traceable to the JSON (I spot-checked composites, aggregates, ROSI, marginals, and framework-sensitivity; all matched except a systematic 0.1pp downward drift in the marginal-contribution prose, which is self-inflicted because the README claims hand-recomputability). The demo-readiness problems are concentrated in the writing, not the work: (1) the executive summary is stale v1.0 and its '23 open flags' line contradicts the rest of the submission — highest-priority fix; (2) the v1.0/v1.1 dual-model story forces the reader to hold two headline numbers and never commits to one answer to 'how much safer does this make them' — say it once in v1.1 terms and demote v1.0 to an audit appendix; (3) the lede is buried under a wall of process boasts ('24 agents', 'schema-validated') before any result, with no top-of-README bottom-line box; (4) the intro's 'web-verified every parameter' overclaims relative to the candidate's own SOURCES.md caveat; (5) a 7-vs-6 agent-count inconsistency and README length (~30% cuttable, mostly the post-caveats feature-restating sections) read as volume-as-signal. None of these are fatal and the honesty story (R5 red-team-up-then-research-down convergence) is genuinely impressive — but a 10-minute evaluator will hit the exec-summary contradiction and the buried lede first, which sells the rigor short.

opportunities.improvement: Add a one-page 'Board / leadership brief' framing that leads with the two numbers a non-technical board actually acts on — dollar EAL reduction ($1.69M -> $0.53M) and probability of a breach this year (56.7% -> 20.5%) — plus the ROSI %, before any risk IDs or FAIR mechanics. The analytics already exist in outputs/08 and the executive summary; this is repackaging, not new modeling.
opportunities.addresses_pain: Only 15% of orgs actually quantify cyber risk financially while 88% say it is essential; CISOs/small teams struggle to translate technical risk into board language and boards increasingly demand dollar-denominated, FAIR-style justification for spend.
opportunities.why_high_value: This is the single most visible way the deliverable speaks to how a 3-person team with no security lead has to sell spend upward. The dollars/probability/ROSI content is already computed and research-calibrated; surfacing it as a standalone leadership artifact (vs. buried mid-README) directly demonstrates the persona's hardest recurring task with near-zero build risk.

opportunities.improvement: Explicitly reframe the existing 28 'quick wins' and the SOC 2 evidence matrix as an 'evidence-per-effort' view: for each quick win / control, name the exact artifact it produces for the auditor (already partly in ROADMAP's 'Evidence the control produces' column) and tag which are hours-not-weeks. Add a short callout that risk-reduction work and Type I evidence are the same work, so a stretched team does it once.
opportunities.addresses_pain: Manual SOC 2 evidence collection consumes 40-80 hours per quarter and readiness runs 3-6 months, competing directly with normal operations for a lean 1-3 person team; teams fail Type II on evidence gaps, not missing policies.
opportunities.why_high_value: The deliverable already maps every mitigation to Trust Services Criteria and lists evidence artifacts, but frames it as 'dual-use' in passing. Making evidence-yield a first-class, per-control lens turns the roadmap into something the IT Manager can hand an auditor, directly answering the persona's biggest time sink with content already in ROADMAP.md.

opportunities.improvement: Add a short 'why not just buy a GRC/compliance-automation tool' positioning note that acknowledges the tooling-sprawl reality (75% of orgs run 20+ security tools; 29% of SMBs spend <5% of IT budget on security) and positions this pipeline as the prioritization/decision layer that sits on top of whatever tools they already own (Entra, Defender, GitHub, no new SIEM required — the KRIs already use native sources).
opportunities.addresses_pain: SMBs face acute tool sprawl and budget scrutiny, want integration and automation over more features, and lean teams cannot add another platform; buyers ask 'do I need yet another tool?'
opportunities.why_high_value: A hiring evaluator and a Meridian-like buyer will both immediately wonder how this differs from Vanta/Drata/Secureframe. The deliverable's genuine differentiator — native-source KRIs requiring no SIEM, and it being a reasoning/prioritization layer rather than a data-collection tool — is real but never stated against the tool-sprawl pain. A few sentences closes that gap and shows persona awareness.

opportunities.improvement: Add a brief 'operating this between audits' note to ROADMAP.md: the pipeline is re-runnable quarterly (args.risks), the KRIs have monthly review cadence and named owners — position this explicitly as a defense against SOC 2 control drift (the space between audits where lean teams silently regress on access reviews, offboarding, and monitoring).
opportunities.addresses_pain: Control drift between audits is a top cause of Type II failure; lean 1-3 person teams cannot sustain the manual cadence, and missed access reviews / offboarding are named drift categories that the deliverable's R5 and KRIs already touch.
opportunities.why_high_value: The re-runnable design and monthly-KRI cadence already exist but are pitched as generic 'operationally useful' features. Naming the specific pain they defend against (drift/regression between a 3-6 month Type I and a 12-month Type II window) shows the deliverable understands the persona's lifecycle, not just a one-time assessment, at essentially zero build cost.

opportunities.improvement: In the R5 (offboarding) narrative, anchor the exposure to current external data — e.g. 40% of departing employees retain access to at least one app, ~91% of former-employee tokens can remain active, 65% of apps sit outside IT control — to make the 'manual, ticket-driven, contractors outside SSO' scenario land as a documented industry failure rather than a hypothetical for this one company.
opportunities.addresses_pain: Offboarding/deprovisioning is where SMB security demonstrably breaks down: only ~14% have systematic SaaS deprovisioning, a third-plus of ex-employees retain access, and app sprawl leaves most revocation incomplete — exactly the R5 scenario.
opportunities.why_high_value: R5 is the deliverable's weakest-ROI risk (13% ROSI) and its narrative currently reads as company-specific speculation. Two or three cited industry figures (the research pass already gathers similar data for other risks) make R5 credibly a systemic problem worth funding for SOC 2 evidence value, tightening the one risk a skeptical reviewer is most likely to push back on.

pain_points.pain: SOC 2 readiness and evidence collection eats a lean IT team's time: readiness runs 3-6 months and manual evidence collection (screenshots, log exports across ~10 systems, spreadsheets) costs on the order of 40-80 hours per quarter, all while competing with normal operations. Teams fail Type II on evidence gaps, not missing policies.
pain_points.who_feels_it: The IT Manager + 2 sysadmins running Meridian's Type I effort with SOC 2 prep already consuming ~20% of IT capacity and no dedicated compliance/security owner.
pain_points.evidence: 'Most organizations take 2-4 months to get audit-ready because security work competes with normal operations'; readiness is '3 to 6 months.' 'Without automation, teams spend 40-80 hours per quarter manually documenting these tests'; evidence 'lives in a dozen different systems.' 'Lean security teams of 1-3 people cannot sustain manual evidence collection.'
pain_points.how_or_gap: ROADMAP.md maps every mitigation to Trust Services Criteria and names the evidence artifact each control produces ('SOC 2 dual-use'), and the SOC 2 evidence matrix was audited against AICPA TSC text. This tells the team WHICH evidence each control yields, so risk work doubles as audit prep. Gap: it does not collect or automate the evidence itself (screenshots, log pulls) — appropriate for a demo, but the labor sink remains. Worth closing only as framing (see opportunity: evidence-per-effort view), not as a build.

pain_points.pain: Alert fatigue with no SIEM: native M365/Defender tooling floods a small team with high-volume, low-context alerts and false positives, so real threats get missed and the team has no capacity to triage sign-in anomalies. SMBs have no active security responder ~33% of the time.
pain_points.who_feels_it: Meridian's 3-person IT team with Defender AV on defaults, no EDR monitoring, and no SIEM — explicitly called out as unable to triage sign-in anomalies at scale.
pain_points.evidence: 'SMBs often experience alert fatigue due to the high volume of alerts... many false positives, leading to important alerts being overlooked.' 'You get plenty of data but not enough context... teams spend all their time chasing false positives or... miss real threats entirely.' SMBs 'have no active security responders for 33% of the time.'
pain_points.how_or_gap: The deliverable designs KRIs sourced from native Azure/M365 signals (Entra ID Protection risk detections, WAF logs, Secret Protection dashboard) 'no SIEM required,' and R4's mitigation adds Defender for Business EDR with automated investigation to cut manual triage. It reduces the monitoring burden by design but does not solve alert triage/correlation itself. Gap is acceptable for a demo; the no-SIEM-native-source stance should be stated more explicitly as a deliberate answer to this pain.

pain_points.pain: Small teams struggle to justify security spend to leadership/board because they cannot translate technical risk into dollars: 88% of executives say measuring cyber risk is essential but only ~15% actually quantify financial impact, and boards increasingly demand FAIR/NIST-style quantified, dollar-denominated business cases.
pain_points.who_feels_it: Whoever at Meridian (IT Manager / CTO) has to take a security budget to leadership and the board with no security engineer to build the case.
pain_points.evidence: 'Nearly 9 out of 10 executives (88%) agree that measuring cyber risk is essential... but only 15% actually measure the financial impact.' 'Boards will require quantifiable cyber risk assessments, forcing CISOs to adopt CRQ frameworks such as FAIR and NIST 800-30.' 'Organizations are moving away from blank-check security budgets.'
pain_points.how_or_gap: This is the deliverable's strongest fit. It uses a FAIR-lite ordinal framework, computes P(>=1 material breach) 56.7%->20.5%, expected annual loss $1.69M->$0.53M, and per-mitigation ROSI (portfolio 221%), all in dollars and probabilities with an executive summary and 'what to do first' ordered by marginal risk reduction. It directly hands leadership the quantified business case the persona otherwise lacks. Only gap: the board-facing framing is buried in a dense README rather than a standalone leadership brief (see opportunity).

pain_points.pain: Security tool sprawl under budget pressure: 75% of orgs run 20+ security tools, SMBs overspend 15-30% on overlapping/underused software, 29% of SMBs still allocate <5% of IT spend to security, and what teams want is integration and automation, not more tools.
pain_points.who_feels_it: Meridian leadership deciding whether to buy yet another platform (SIEM, GRC automation, PAM) versus using tools they already license (Entra, Defender, Intune, GitHub).
pain_points.evidence: '75% of organizations use more than 20 cybersecurity tools, and nearly 30% use over 50.' '29% still allocate less than 5% of their IT spend to security.' 'The overwhelming demand wasn't for more features - it was for better integration (61%) and automation (48%).'
pain_points.how_or_gap: Mitigations deliberately lean on tools the company already owns or minimally uplifts (Entra Conditional Access, Defender for Business, Intune, GitHub Secret Protection, native WAF) and KRIs use native data sources with 'no SIEM required'; feasibility agents flag every licensing trap (GitHub Enterprise SSO, E5 vs standalone P2). This restraint directly respects the sprawl/budget pain but is never stated against it. Gap is framing-only (see positioning opportunity).

pain_points.pain: Offboarding/deprovisioning is where SMB security breaks down: only ~14% of companies have systematic SaaS deprovisioning, ~40% of departing employees retain access to at least one app, ~91% of former-employee tokens can stay active, and up to 65% of apps sit outside IT control, so revocation is chronically incomplete across SSO, sessions, and shadow IT.
pain_points.who_feels_it: Meridian's IT team running manual, ticket-driven offboarding across Entra, ~15 SSO apps, Azure, GitHub, and direct contractor Postgres logins outside SSO (risk R5).
pain_points.evidence: 'Only 14% of surveyed companies have systems and processes for SaaS deprovisioning.' '40% of departing employees retain access to at least one business application.' 'The 2025 State of NHIs... reports that 91% of former employee tokens remain active after offboarding.' 'Up to 65% of apps are outside IT control.'
pain_points.how_or_gap: R5 targets exactly this: Entra ID as the single revocation point, Entra auth on PostgreSQL to kill standing local logins, SCIM for priority SaaS, an automated leaver workflow, and a leaver-SLA KRI (accounts disabled within 24h, 100%). It also flags contractor-Postgres cleanup as a Wave-0 quick win. The mechanism is well matched; the narrative would land harder with the external stats above (see opportunity).

pain_points.pain: No dedicated security hire means IT staff wear the security hat without specialized training, driving burnout (85% of IT pros report burnout, 90% say it worsened) and a skills gap that hits SMBs hardest; teams now say they need skills/leverage more than headcount.
pain_points.who_feels_it: Meridian's IT Manager + 2 sysadmins covering security with no security engineer, while also carrying SOC 2 prep.
pain_points.evidence: 'IT staff may be expected to handle cybersecurity tasks in addition to their regular duties... may not have specialized training.' 'A Sophos study revealed that 85% of IT professionals experience burnout, with 90% reporting... it has worsened.' 'Respondents... prioritized the need for critical skills as more important than the need for more people.'
pain_points.how_or_gap: The entire premise — an agentic pipeline that does FAIR-style prioritization, adversarial challenge, mitigation design, feasibility pricing, and quantification — supplies exactly the specialized security-analyst reasoning the 3-person team lacks, and the feasibility agents role-play the team's real capacity constraints (effort raised ~40-50%, calendar >> person-weeks). This is the deliverable's core value proposition for the persona. No gap worth closing for a demo.

pain_points.pain: Leaked/hardcoded secrets are near-universal and long-lived: ~32-35% of internal/private repositories contain at least one hardcoded secret, exposure keeps rising (28.65M new public secrets in 2025), and ~64-70% of leaked credentials remain valid years later — the exact R3 scenario of historical .env files and long-lived service-principal secrets.
pain_points.who_feels_it: Meridian's ~60 engineers and 3-person IT team with historical connection strings in private repos and long-lived Contributor service-principal secrets in GitHub Actions, and no security staff watching for exposure (risk R3, ranked #1).
pain_points.evidence: '35% of private repositories contain exposed credentials'; '32.2% of internal repos contain at least one hardcoded secret.' '28.65 million new hardcoded secrets were added to public GitHub commits in 2025... a 34% increase.' 'Nearly 70% of credentials confirmed as valid in 2022 were still valid in January 2025... above 64% when retested in January 2026.'
pain_points.how_or_gap: R3 is the top-ranked risk with a full mitigation package: GitHub Actions OIDC workload-identity federation to delete long-lived secrets, GitHub Secret Protection (push protection + history triage), Key Vault, least-privilege RBAC, immutable backups — plus a Wave-0 quick win to revoke historical .env secrets immediately and a KRI targeting zero long-lived service-principal secrets. The research pass already cites GitGuardian 2026 to calibrate this baseline. Fully addressed.

pain_points.pain: SOC 2 control drift between audits: controls documented at audit time silently degrade (missed access reviews, untracked changes, incomplete evidence), and a first Type I covering 3-6 months is followed by a Type II looking back 12 months, right when early discipline fades — lean teams cannot sustain the cadence manually.
pain_points.who_feels_it: Meridian post-Type-I, heading toward Type II with the same 3-person team and no continuous-compliance owner.
pain_points.evidence: 'Second-year audits tend to go wrong in the space between audits... drift has crept in.' 'Control drift exposes organizations... for months between audits... as security controls... gradually degrade through routine operations.' 'Lean security teams of 1-3 people cannot sustain manual evidence collection.'
pain_points.how_or_gap: The pipeline is re-runnable quarterly (accepts an updated register via args.risks) and ships per-risk KRIs with native data sources, targets, owners, and a monthly review cadence — the ingredients for catching drift (e.g. R5's 24h leaver-SLA KRI, R3's zero-standing-secrets KRI). But this is framed as a generic 're-runnable by design' feature, not as a drift defense. Gap is framing-only and cheap to close (see opportunity); building actual continuous monitoring would be scope creep for a demo.

data.summary: The deliverable is a point-in-time risk prioritization and quantification artifact, and it maps unusually well onto the DECISION-MAKING pains of this exact persona (a 200-person SaaS, 3-person IT team, no security engineer, mid-SOC-2-Type-I). Its strongest fit is the hardest recurring task these teams have: justifying security spend to leadership. Only ~15% of orgs actually quantify cyber risk in dollars though 88% call it essential, and this deliverable hands them a FAIR-lite model with dollar EAL ($1.69M -> $0.53M), breach probability (56.7% -> 20.5%), per-mitigation ROSI, and a marginal-reduction sequencing order — research-calibrated against DBIR/GitGuardian/Sophos. It also directly addresses the top current secrets-sprawl problem (R3/OIDC), the offboarding-breakdown problem (R5/Entra single revocation point + 24h leaver KRI), and supplies the specialized security-analyst reasoning a no-security-hire team lacks, while feasibility agents model the team's real 3-person capacity and licensing traps — respecting acute SMB tool-sprawl and budget constraints (29% of SMBs spend <5% of IT budget on security). Where it is only PARTIAL is the OPERATIONAL/RECURRING grind rather than the plan: it maps controls to SOC 2 evidence but does not collect that evidence (40-80 hrs/quarter of manual work remains); it designs no-SIEM native-source KRIs but does not solve alert triage; and it is re-runnable quarterly with monthly KRI cadence but does not frame that as a defense against control drift between Type I and Type II. All of these partial gaps are appropriate to leave unbuilt for a 3-4h demo. The highest-value, lowest-scope-creep moves are therefore framing/repackaging of content that already exists: (1) a standalone board/leadership brief leading with dollars-probability-ROSI; (2) recasting the existing quick-wins and TSC matrix as an 'evidence-per-effort' view that names the auditor artifact each control yields; (3) an explicit 'why not just buy another tool' positioning against tool sprawl (native sources, no SIEM); (4) naming the re-runnable pipeline + KRIs as a drift defense between audits; and (5) anchoring the R5 offboarding narrative in current external stats (~40% retain access, ~91% of ex-employee tokens stay active). Each is Small effort, low risk, and makes the deliverable visibly speak to the persona's daily reality rather than only its assessment.

pain_points.pain: A 3-person IT team with no security engineer must personally own SOC 2 implementation, technical controls, and evidence collection on top of their day jobs — every top risk needs a named owner and a hard deadline or the assessment becomes shelfware. Risk registers without owners are shelfware.
pain_points.who_feels_it: IT Manager / CTO acting as de-facto security lead at a 200-person SaaS mid-SOC-2
pain_points.evidence: 'SOC 2 project manager owns timeline... Security lead implements technical controls (often CTO, VP Eng)... With only 3 people and no dedicated security engineer, these responsibilities fall on already overextended team members.' And separately: 'Risk registers without owners are shelfware. Assign each top risk to a business owner, set a firm deadline and publish the owner in the board one-page.'
pain_points.how_or_gap: The mitigation table, ROADMAP sequencing, and KRI table all assign named owners (IT Manager / Head of Eng) and per-KRI accountability with day-90/120 targets; the feasibility agent re-priced effort for exactly this 3-person team. Well covered.

pain_points.pain: SOC 2 for a cash-constrained startup costs $30k-$150k and consumes 3-6 months; teams need risk spend to double as audit evidence rather than being a separate cost center.
pain_points.who_feels_it: Founder/CTO funding both risk remediation and a SOC 2 Type I in ~6 months on a $20M ARR budget
pain_points.evidence: 'SOC 2 cost in 2025 averages between $30,000 - $150,000 (including hidden costs)... For a startup with limited cash and a tiny IT team, this represents a substantial investment.'
pain_points.how_or_gap: The SOC 2 evidence matrix in ROADMAP.md maps every mitigation to Trust Services Criteria (audited against AICPA TSC text, two dropped/two added), explicitly making risk spend double as audit evidence. This is a genuine differentiator, not filler.

pain_points.pain: Boards and evaluators increasingly reject the ordinal risk matrix ('high/medium/low', 1-5 scores) as pseudo-quantification; credible work must express exposure in financial terms with explicit uncertainty rather than false precision.
pain_points.who_feels_it: Technical evaluator and notional Meridian leadership/board consuming the risk output
pain_points.evidence: 'Many within the profession mistake numeric ordinal values (e.g., 1-5 scales, CVSS scores) as quantification' and 'When evidence is weak, the right response is wider uncertainty, not forced precision. FAIR makes uncertainty explicit so decision makers can see what is measured, what is inferred, and where better data would genuinely improve confidence.'
pain_points.how_or_gap: The pipeline is FAIR-lite ORDINAL for prioritization (L x I, 1-25) but then adds a real probabilistic layer (baselines/effectiveness as low/mode/high, Monte Carlo with P5-P95 bands, tornado, correlated variant) and is unusually honest about uncertainty. Gap: the money layer stops at EAL/ROSI point estimates and a probability of >=1 breach; it never plots a loss-exceedance curve or states a dollar risk-appetite threshold, which is the specific board artifact 2025-26 CRQ guidance centers on.

pain_points.pain: GRC/security buyers in 2025-26 are actively skeptical of 'agentic AI' autonomy claims; the winners are those who ship reliable agents on realistic scope with transparent error metrics and honest disclosure of limits, because multi-agent hallucination cascades and citation failures have burned buyers (e.g., KPMG pulling its agentic-AI report over disputed claims and citation problems).
pain_points.who_feels_it: Technical hiring evaluator judging the 'agentic AI' framing; a CISO/GRC buyer imagining production use
pain_points.evidence: '67% of GRC buyers say vendor autonomy claims are overstated or misleading... The winners will be the ones who delivered reliable agents on realistic scope, with transparent error metrics, and honest communication about what they can't yet do.' And: 'On June 13, 2026, KPMG pulled its report... after GPTZero documented widespread citation problems.'
pain_points.how_or_gap: This is the deliverable's strongest area: deterministic-code-not-model arithmetic, schema-forced JSON, independent scorers/estimators, adversarial challengers allowed to say 'upheld', a first-class HUMAN_REVIEW_FLAGS artifact, a replay harness proving byte-identical recompute, and a research-verification pass with a per-source SOURCES.md. It embodies the 'transparent, auditable, human-override, honest-about-limits' posture buyers demand — it just doesn't summarize its own reliability as headline error metrics.

pain_points.pain: Agentic-AI trust in 2026 hinges on decision provenance and answering 'what data led to this action?' — process signals (provenance coverage, trace, permission conformance) matter as much as the final answer, and unverified upstream context is the top failure mode.
pain_points.who_feels_it: Evaluator assessing whether the pipeline is trustworthy; GRC governance reviewer
pain_points.evidence: 'Evaluation should combine outcome measures with process signals including trace minimization, permission conformance, and provenance coverage.
