Decision provenance graphs make agent behaviors interpretable.' And: 'Every orchestration framework assumes the context fed to agents is trustworthy, but none of them verify it... Teams may lose the ability to answer: What data led to this action?'
pain_points.how_or_gap: The full factor-level audit trail (3 lenses x 5 risks x 4 factors with falsifiable reasons), preserved raw estimates + red-team issues + revision change-logs, and the research-verifier pass that treats upstream parameters as untrusted-until-cited directly answer 'what led to this number.' Strong. Minor gap: there is no single provenance/lineage diagram or coverage metric surfacing this as a governance property.

pain_points.pain: Cyber insurers in 2025-26 have moved from checklists to proof, and now specifically require MFA, EDR, and immutable/tested backups as the controls that most move premiums and claim-payability; failing to match documented controls to reality can void claims. For an SMB SaaS this is both a cost lever and a residual-risk (risk-transfer) reality that risk work should address.
pain_points.who_feels_it: CTO/finance at Meridian weighing residual risk, insurance, and control spend
pain_points.evidence: 'MFA, EDR, and immutable backups are the three controls that move the needle most with underwriters... Underwriting moved from checklists to proof, and insurers increasingly require evidence, not self-attestation... sometimes insurers may deny a claim if they determine your controls did not match what you documented.'
pain_points.how_or_gap: The top three mitigations (R1 phishing-resistant MFA, R4 EDR, R3 immutable backups) are exactly the three controls underwriters check, but the deliverable never connects the dots: no mention of cyber-insurance eligibility/premium impact, no risk-transfer line in the residual-risk story, and the ROSI framing treats all residual risk as retained. Worth closing — it is a cheap, persona-perfect framing that reframes residual risk as insurable and control spend as premium-reducing.

pain_points.pain: Boards want risk reporting tied to a formally approved risk appetite/tolerance, yet even at mature orgs boards consume cyber-risk information in fewer than half of cases — the reporting fails to connect exposure to an appetite threshold the board actually signed off on.
pain_points.who_feels_it: Meridian leadership/board; the evaluator judging board-readiness
pain_points.evidence: 'Nearly all respondents have defined risk appetite and tolerance levels that are formally approved by the boards, [but] boards consume cyber risk information in less than half of the participating organizations.' Boards need 'strategic summary showing overall risk trajectory and how it compares to risk appetite.'
pain_points.how_or_gap: The executive summary and dashboard give a board-legible headline (65.7%->25.5%, fund R1+R3) and EAL $1.69M->$0.53M, but never state a dollar or probability risk-appetite/tolerance threshold to judge residual risk against ('is 20.5% / $0.53M EAL acceptable?'). Adding one stated, ratifiable appetite line converts a good summary into a board-decision instrument.

pain_points.pain: Risk assessments become shelfware unless every gap becomes a tracked, budgeted action item with quick wins that can start immediately, and unless the work is re-run continuously rather than as a one-off; infrequent assessment leaves gaps and monthly validation measurably reduces breaches.
pain_points.who_feels_it: IT Manager operationalizing the assessment; leadership wanting momentum before the SOC 2 window
pain_points.evidence: 'It should not spend the rest of its life as a bullet point in a forgotten PDF.' And '67% of organizations said infrequent testing... leaving gaps in assessment is an issue. Organizations that run exposure validation testing at least once a month reported a 20% reduction in breaches.'
pain_points.how_or_gap: 28 sub-week quick wins, a 30/60/90 cut, phased waves with a live-hole jumping the queue, KRIs with monthly review cadence and native Azure/M365 data sources, and an explicitly re-runnable pipeline (args.risks, quarterly re-run + diff) all directly answer the shelfware and continuity concerns. Well covered.

opportunities.improvement: Add a one-page board view that plots a loss-exceedance curve (probability of exceeding $X loss in 12 months, before vs after mitigation) and states a single ratifiable risk-appetite threshold (e.g., 'board tolerance: <10% annual chance of a >$2M loss event'), showing where baseline and residual sit relative to it. The inputs already exist (impact-band midpoints, per-risk low/mode/high probabilities, the Monte Carlo distribution) — this is a re-projection of data you already computed, not new modeling.
opportunities.addresses_pain: Boards reject ordinal matrices and want exposure in dollars against an approved appetite; 2025-26 CRQ board guidance centers the loss-exceedance curve and the average / 1-in-10 / 1-in-100 loss points; boards consume risk info less than half the time partly because it is not tied to a tolerance they signed off on.
opportunities.why_high_value: It is the single most-cited board artifact in current CRQ practice and the deliverable's most conspicuous gap: it already has a Monte Carlo distribution and EAL but never turns them into a loss-in-dollars exceedance view or names an appetite threshold. Converting probability-of-any-breach into 'here is our tail loss vs. what the board said it will tolerate' is exactly the senior move that separates a quant exercise from a board-decision instrument — and it reuses existing numbers, so scope-creep risk is minimal.

opportunities.improvement: Add a short 'Residual risk & risk transfer' subsection: note that the top three funded controls (phishing-resistant MFA, EDR, immutable/tested backups) are precisely the three controls cyber-insurance underwriters now check as proof (not self-attestation), so the program simultaneously lowers modeled EAL AND improves insurability/premium and claim-payability — and frame the ~20.5% residual / $0.53M EAL explicitly as 'retain vs. transfer' rather than silently retained.
opportunities.addresses_pain: Underwriters moved from checklists to proof and require MFA/EDR/immutable backups; SMBs with documented matching controls pay less and avoid claim denial. The deliverable's residual-risk story treats all leftover risk as retained and never mentions insurance, missing a persona-perfect, decision-relevant framing.
opportunities.why_high_value: This is domain judgment that a strong evaluator will notice is absent: quantifying residual risk without addressing risk transfer is incomplete for an SMB, and the mapping is almost free because the three flagship controls already match the underwriter checklist verbatim. It reframes control spend as premium-reducing and closes the loop on the ROSI story (retain-vs-transfer), signaling the author thinks like a security leader, not just a modeler.

opportunities.improvement: Surface a compact 'pipeline reliability scorecard' at the top of the README/dashboard: a handful of headline trust metrics the run already produced — e.g., % of numeric parameters research-verified vs. still expert-judgment, count of adversarial challenges raised/upheld/refuted, schema-validation retry rate, replay-harness PASS, and count of open vs. worked human-review flags — so a skeptical agentic-AI buyer sees transparent error/coverage metrics up front instead of having to reconstruct them from prose.
opportunities.addresses_pain: 67% of GRC buyers call agentic-AI autonomy claims overstated; winners ship 'transparent error metrics and honest communication about what they can't yet do', and trustworthy-agent evaluation combines outcomes with process signals like provenance coverage. The substance exists throughout the deliverable but is never summarized as headline reliability metrics.
opportunities.why_high_value: The deliverable already does the hard, trustworthy things (deterministic arithmetic, schema validation, adversarial pairs, research calibration, replay harness, flags-as-artifact) but buries them in narrative; after the June-2026 KPMG citation-failure incident, leading with quantified reliability/coverage is exactly the differentiator skeptical buyers reward. It is pure re-presentation of existing run data, so it raises the ceiling on the 'agentic AI' framing with near-zero new work and no scope creep.

opportunities.improvement: Promote a single lineage/provenance diagram (or one-line-per-number 'where did this come from' index) that traces each headline number from seed register -> agent output -> deterministic computation -> research-verified source, answering 'what data led to this action?' at a glance for the top ~5 numbers (composite ranking, aggregate P(breach), each key parameter's citation).
opportunities.addresses_pain: 2026 agentic-AI trust literature says process signals and decision provenance ('what data led to this action?') matter as much as outputs, and unverified upstream context is the top failure mode; evaluators increasingly want provenance coverage as a governance property, not buried audit JSON.
opportunities.why_high_value: The full provenance already exists across outputs/01-09 and SOURCES.md, but it is spread across many files; a single traceability view makes the pipeline's biggest strength legible in ten seconds and directly answers the governance question buyers now ask. Slightly higher effort than the others because it must reconcile numbers across files, but it converts existing rigor into a visible, senior-level governance artifact.

data.summary: The deliverable is already best-in-class on most axes for this persona: it correctly separates model-estimated parameters from deterministic computation (the #1 thing agentic-AI/GRC buyers demand after 2026's hallucination incidents), ships a defensible-assumptions log, a first-class human-review-flags artifact, an ATT&CK kill-chain mapping, an AICPA-audited SOC 2 evidence matrix, KRIs with native data sources, a replay harness, and a research-calibration pass with per-source citations. These are genuine differentiators, not table stakes, and they map cleanly onto documented pains of a 3-person-IT, no-security-engineer, mid-SOC-2 SaaS. The ceiling-raising gaps are small and about FRAMING, not more modeling. Highest value (all low-scope-creep because they re-present data the pipeline already produced): (1) a one-page board loss-exceedance-curve view stating a ratifiable dollar/probability risk-appetite threshold — the single board artifact 2025-26 CRQ practice centers on and the deliverable's most visible omission; (2) a residual-risk / cyber-insurance risk-transfer framing, since the three flagship controls (phishing-resistant MFA, EDR, immutable backups) are exactly what underwriters now require as proof — a persona-perfect, nearly-free addition; and (3) a top-level pipeline-reliability scorecard (parameters verified vs. expert-judgment, challenges raised/upheld, schema-retry rate, replay PASS, flags open vs. worked) that leads with the transparent error metrics skeptical agentic-AI buyers reward. A medium-effort fourth: a single provenance/lineage view answering 'what data led to this number.' None require re-running the pipeline or expanding scope; each signals senior security-leadership judgment rather than additional machinery.

action_plan.top_theme: The submission's entire competitive edge is rigor and honest self-assessment, yet its most-read leadership artifacts quietly contradict their own audited sources — the executive summary and README ship stale v1.0 numbers and an un-reconciled "23 open flags" line, while three source-of-truth files (advanced_analytics EAL, outputs/04 SOC 2, ROADMAP ATT&CK) each carry a defect a domain expert spots in seconds. Fix the small set of consistency/labeling defects so the artifacts agree with each other, add 2-3 framing-only moves (board loss/appetite view, insurance risk-transfer, reliability scorecard) that reuse data already computed, and protect the verified-strong core (mutation-proof replay, 0.40-correlation Monte Carlo, feasibility pushback) untouched. The win is a SHARPER deliverable that says one number, defends it, and never contradicts itself — not a bigger one.

must_fix.item: Reconcile the executive summary body to v1.1: replace the '23 open human-review flags' sentence with '23 flags raised, of which 2 remain genuinely open after the research pass (notification-duty materiality; concurrent-delivery capacity)', and requote the headline as ~57%->~21% (or add a one-line pointer that the editorial note's v1.1 figures supersede the body). The body currently declares 23 OPEN and headlines 65.7%/25.5%/R1 11.7pp, directly contradicting its own top-of-file editorial note and every other doc.
must_fix.why: This is the single file a busy leader actually reads, and it contradicts its own header and HUMAN_REVIEW_FLAGS.md on the most reputation-sensitive number in the doc (how much to distrust it). For a rigor-first submission this is the first eye-roll a sharp evaluator hits.
must_fix.source_finding: Writing critique (high sev) + Security critique headline-mismatch finding

must_fix.item: Fix the marginal-contribution prose to match the JSON: README line 160 and the exec summary say R1 -11.7 / R2 -5.5 / R5 -2.1, but outputs/08 has 11.8 / 5.6 / 2.2. Regenerate the table straight from the JSON (R3=6.0 and R4=3.9 already match) or state a rounding convention.
must_fix.why: The core credibility claim is 'you can recompute every number by hand from the JSON.' A systematic 0.1 downward drift on the headline 'largest single lever' number means the prose disagrees with its own artifact at exactly the spot an evaluator spot-checks. Self-inflicted.

must_fix.item: Compute EAL on the triangular mean (low+mode+high)/3 (or reuse the MC mean the same script already produces) instead of p['b'][1] (the mode). The script runs a 100k-iteration Monte Carlo, then computes 'Expected' Annual Loss on the mode of an admittedly right-skewed triangular, understating portfolio EAL ~13.6% (up to ~18% on R4). If not recomputing, rename away from 'Expected' and state the mode-point-estimate bias inline.
must_fix.why: The one place the most numerate part of the submission does something a quant evaluator reads as 'doesn't know E[X] uses the mean, not the mode' — with the mean sitting right there for free. Highest-value quant credibility fix; does not change the fund-R1-and-R3 decision.

must_fix.item: Regenerate outputs/04_mitigations.json soc2_criteria to the audited set: drop CC6.7 (and CC7.3) from R1, drop CC9.2 from R5 — so the source JSON agrees with the corrected ROADMAP matrix and outputs/09. If regenerating is too invasive, add one line to ROADMAP noting outputs/04 holds the pre-audit set.
must_fix.why: Auditability is the pitch, and the SOC 2 mapping is the most auditor-facing artifact. A reviewer who diffs the corrected matrix against its own source JSON finds them disagreeing on the exact criteria — the drift lands where it hurts most.

must_fix.item: Fix the ATT&CK T1213 misfit for SQL-database exfil on R2 and R5: drop 'T1213 Data from Information Repositories' (scoped by MITRE to SharePoint/Confluence/wikis/code repos, not a backend relational DB) and either map collection to the in-chain app-layer technique noting ATT&CK lacks a precise 'dump SQL DB' Collection ID. Keep R3's T1213.003 (Code Repositories) — that one is correct. Stop claiming 'all 22 verified' if this fit was never scrutinized.
must_fix.why: A security architect spots T1213-for-a-SQL-dump immediately; under the bold 'all 22 verified against live pages' banner it reads as verification theater and undercuts the whole research-calibration story.

must_fix.item: Add two free E3-native quick wins to R1 that the submission's own research (outputs/09, SOURCES.md) says are needed: (1) a Conditional Access policy blocking the OAuth device-code authorization-grant flow, and (2) restrict user app-consent to verified publishers / low-impact permissions with an admin-consent workflow. Both close the Storm-2372 / consent-phishing bypass that defeats phishing-resistant MFA.
must_fix.why: The pipeline diagnosed the exact bypass in its own research and then didn't prescribe the fix. A vCISO reviewer calls that out as 'you knew and still left it open' — and the fix is $0 and directly closes the documented gap.

must_fix.item: Reconcile the second-pass agent count and soften 'web-verified every parameter': say '7-agent pass (6 verifiers + 1 assessor)' consistently (README:5 says 7, lines 172/210/228 say 6), and change 'web-verified every parameter against current primary sources' to 'AI-researched every parameter against current primary sources (human click-through recommended; one parameter has no literature and stays labeled expert judgment)' to match SOURCES.md line 66.
must_fix.why: Two different counts for the same thing on the same page, plus an intro claim bolder than the candidate's own caveat three files deep, plant doubt in a rigor-first submission. One-word/one-clause edits.

high_value_adds.item: Add a top-of-README 'Bottom line' box (3-4 lines) and lead outputs/07 with it: top risk R3, biggest lever R1, ~57%->~21% breach probability, EAL $1.69M->$0.53M, ROSI ~221%, fund R1+R3 now — stated once in v1.1 terms, with v1.0 demoted to an explicit audit-trail appendix. Cut v1.0 numbers out of the exec summary body entirely.
high_value_adds.pain_addressed: Boards demand dollar/probability-denominated business cases and the buried-lede problem: the actual finding doesn't appear until README line 101, behind a wall of '24 agents / schema-validated' process boasts; and the dual-model story forces the reader to hold two headline numbers and never commits to one answer to 'how much safer does this make them?'
high_value_adds.expected_impact: Resolves the #1 skim failure (evaluator hits methodology before any result) AND the dual-model confusion in one move; turns a good summary into an executive-ready deliverable. Pure repackaging of existing numbers.

high_value_adds.item: Add a one-page board view that plots a loss-exceedance curve (probability of exceeding $X loss in 12 months, baseline vs residual) and states a single ratifiable risk-appetite threshold (e.g. 'board tolerance: <10% annual chance of a >$2M loss event'), showing where baseline and residual sit against it. Inputs already exist (impact-band midpoints, per-risk low/mode/high, the Monte Carlo distribution).
high_value_adds.pain_addressed: Boards reject ordinal matrices and want exposure in dollars against an APPROVED appetite; 2025-26 CRQ board practice centers the loss-exceedance curve, and boards consume cyber-risk info <half the time partly because it isn't tied to a tolerance they signed off on. This is the deliverable's most conspicuous board-artifact gap.
high_value_adds.expected_impact: Converts a quant exercise into a board-decision instrument — the senior move a strong evaluator notices is absent. Re-projects data already computed; low scope-creep.

high_value_adds.item: Add a short 'Residual risk & risk transfer' note: the three flagship funded controls (R1 phishing-resistant MFA, R4 EDR, R3 immutable/tested backups) are exactly the three controls cyber-insurance underwriters now require as proof, so the program lowers modeled EAL AND improves insurability/premium/claim-payability. Frame the ~20.5% / $0.53M residual explicitly as retain-vs-transfer rather than silently retained.
high_value_adds.pain_addressed: Underwriters moved from checklists to proof and specifically require MFA/EDR/immutable backups; the deliverable quantifies residual risk but never addresses risk transfer — incomplete for an SMB, and the three controls already match the underwriter checklist verbatim.
high_value_adds.expected_impact: Signals security-leadership judgment (not just modeling) and closes the ROSI loop; near-free because the control mapping already exists. A senior reviewer notices its absence.

high_value_adds.item: Surface a compact 'pipeline reliability scorecard' at the top of README/dashboard: % of numeric parameters research-verified vs. still expert-judgment, count of adversarial challenges raised/upheld/refuted, schema-validation retry rate, replay-harness PASS, and open-vs-worked human-review flags. Pure re-presentation of run data that already exists.
high_value_adds.pain_addressed: 67% of GRC buyers call agentic-AI autonomy claims overstated; post-June-2026 KPMG citation-failure incident, winners lead with transparent error/coverage metrics and honest limits. The deliverable does all the trustworthy things but buries them in prose instead of headline metrics.
high_value_adds.expected_impact: Raises the ceiling on the 'agentic AI' framing with near-zero new work; leads with exactly the transparency skeptical buyers reward, and reinforces the honest-about-limits brand.

high_value_adds.item: Extend the impact-weight sweep to include secondary-dominant weights (add 0.30-0.40 primary) and report whether R3 still leads; anchor CC9.2 (dropped from R5) to a one-line 'R6 candidate: third-party/vendor breach (TPRM)' stub with one KRI (% of the ~15 SSO vendors with a current SOC 2 report on file).
high_value_adds.pain_addressed: Evaluator expectation: the one-directional sweep (0.50->0.70 only) presents 'top risk stable' as robustness while never testing the secondary-dominant view R2/R3's own loss bands suggest matters most; and for a B2B SaaS reselling through ~15 SSO apps, TPRM is expected to at least appear — flagging the gap then dropping the one criterion (CC9.2) that fits reads as oversight.
high_value_adds.expected_impact: Converts a one-sided robustness claim into a genuine two-sided one (R3 very likely still leads) and turns a named-but-untreated coverage gap into a tracked one — both pre-empt obvious reviewer questions cheaply.

do_not_do.item: Do NOT refactor the replay harness's regex meta-strip into a full 'export a runnable async function' architecture, and do NOT reseed/rework the Monte Carlo RNG plumbing in a way that could break the verified 0.40-correlation shared-shock construction or the byte-identical reproducibility.
do_not_do.why_scope_creep: The harness is mutation-provable and the correlation model is empirically verified at 0.40 (both are the credibility anchors). The RNG-shared-across-versions and meta-strip fragilities are real craft nits but the OUTPUT is correct and reproducible today; a demo does not need the invasive refactor, and a botched one risks regressing the single most technically credible pieces. At most: a one-line post-strip assertion and a rng.seed(SEED) reset per version — nothing more.

do_not_do.item: Do NOT build actual SOC 2 evidence collection, continuous monitoring, alert-triage/correlation, or a live-loading dashboard that reads the JSON at runtime.
do_not_do.why_scope_creep: These are the PARTIAL-gap pains the research explicitly says to leave unbuilt for a 3-4h demo. The dashboard is intentionally self-contained/CSP-safe and honest about being hand-built; making it live-load would be blocked by CSP anyway. The 40-80hr/quarter evidence grind and drift-defense are worth naming in framing (evidence-per-effort view, 'operating between audits' note), not building.

do_not_do.item: Do NOT add the medium-effort single provenance/lineage diagram reconciling every number across outputs/01-09, and do NOT expand into a 6th fully-scored risk (R6 vendor breach) with its own mitigation package and full FAIR treatment.
do_not_do.why_scope_creep: The lineage diagram is flagged medium-effort/medium scope-creep-risk because it must reconcile numbers across many files — the reliability scorecard (S) delivers 80% of the same 'transparent process signals' value for a fraction of the effort. A full R6 dilutes the tight 5-risk story; a one-line R6-candidate stub + one KRI (already in high_value_adds) is the right dose.

do_not_do.item: Do NOT compute the 'true last-in marginal' contribution or otherwise re-engineer the marginal model to justify the 'marginal_contribution' label.
do_not_do.why_scope_creep: The ordering conclusion (R1 is the biggest lever) survives either way, and the dashboard sub-label already correctly says 'standalone reduction if only this mitigation.' The cheap fix is a one-word rename to 'standalone_reduction'; adding a second parallel computation is precision theater a demo doesn't need.

do_not_do.item: Do NOT trim the README's substantive content or flatten the IT-feasibility pushback and effectiveness discounts into clean 'approved' packages while cutting length.
do_not_do.why_scope_creep: The ~30% length cut should come from the post-caveats feature-restating sections ('Beyond the brief', porting, changelog) — NOT the feasibility objections (Defender-alert-fatigue, employeeLeaveDateTime manual failure, RLS-under-PgBouncer, R3/R5 Postgres collision) or the capped effectiveness ranges. Those are precisely what separate this from the mediocre pile; a cleanup pass that sands them smooth destroys the credibility it's trying to sharpen.

action_plan.demo_narrative: A 3-person IT team at a 200-person SaaS mid-SOC-2, with no security engineer, needs to know which risks to fund first and how to defend that spend to their board. This pipeline runs 24 schema-validated agents (with a second research pass that web-calibrated every parameter and adversarially critiqued its own output) to rank five risks, design right-sized Azure/M365-native mitigations that a real IT manager pushed back on, and quantify the payoff in the language a board acts on: a ~57%->~21% chance of a material breach this year, $1.69M->$0.53M expected annual loss, ~221% ROSI — with a stated risk-appetite threshold and the note that the top three controls are exactly what cyber-insurers now require. Every number is computed by deterministic code, traces back to a cited source or a labeled expert-judgment flag, and survives a mutation-proof replay harness that recomputes it byte-for-byte — and the pipeline is honest about the two things still worth a human's eyes. It's not another GRC tool to buy; it's the prioritization and provenance layer that sits on top of the tools they already own.
